note_data.title: Rates of reaction
keyPoints: **Rate of reaction** is the speed at which reactants are used up or products are formed
For a reaction to occur, particles must **collide with sufficient energy** (activation energy) and correct orientation
Increasing **temperature** increases rate because particles move faster, collide more frequently and with greater energy
Increasing **concentration** of solutions increases rate because more particles in same volume leads to more frequent collisions
Increasing **pressure** of gases increases rate because particles are closer together, leading to more frequent collisions
Decreasing **particle size** (increasing surface area) increases rate because more surface is exposed for collisions
A **catalyst** increases rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
Catalysts are **chemically unchanged** at the end of the reaction and can be reused
Rate can be measured by monitoring **gas volume produced**, **mass lost**, or **time for reaction to complete**

definitions.definition: The speed at which reactants are used up or products are formed
A substance that increases the rate of reaction without being chemically changed
The minimum energy that colliding particles need for a reaction to occur
For a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed observations without explaining why they happen

modelAnswer.question: Explain why increasing the temperature increases the rate of reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
modelAnswer.answer: When temperature increases, particles move faster. This means they collide more frequently. The particles also collide with greater energy, so more collisions have energy above the activation energy.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: collisions have greater energy/more successful collisions

commonMistakes: Never say 'molecules get excited' — always say 'particles move faster with more kinetic energy'
Students often confuse concentration with amount — it's particles per unit volume that matters
Don't say catalysts 'speed up particles' — they provide alternative pathway with lower activation energy
Never say surface area increases collisions — say it exposes more surface for collisions to occur
Students write 'more collisions' without saying they need sufficient energy (activation energy)
examinerTips: Rate of reaction appears in every Paper 2 — often combined with practical skills
Always mention 'activation energy' when explaining temperature effects for full marks
Graph questions are common — know that steeper gradient = faster rate
Catalyst questions often ask for 'chemically unchanged' for the mark
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Energy profile diagram showing activation energy barrier, with and without catalyst. Lower pathway with catalyst demonstrates how it speeds up reaction without changing overall energy change.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how temperature and surface area affect reaction rate using collision theory
N/D 2022 P1 Q8 (2 marks) — multiple choice question on identifying factors that increase rate of reaction between zinc and hydrochloric acid
M/J 2022 P2 Q5(c) (4 marks) — students had to draw and interpret a graph showing effect of catalyst on rate of decomposition of hydrogen peroxide
N/D 2023 P2 Q3(b) (5 marks) — explain why powdered calcium carbonate reacts faster with dilute HCl than marble chips, including particle collision theory

workedExample.question: Explain why increasing temperature increases the rate of reaction between magnesium and dilute hydrochloric acid. Use collision theory in your answer. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: State that increasing temperature increases kinetic energy of particles
Step 2: Explain that particles move faster and collide more frequently
Step 3: Mention that more particles have energy equal to or greater than activation energy
Step 4: Conclude that more successful collisions occur per unit time
workedExample.answer: When temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the reactant particles increases. This means the particles move faster and collide more frequently. Additionally, a greater proportion of particles will have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy needed for the reaction to occur. Therefore, there are more successful collisions per unit time, increasing the rate of reaction.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for kinetic energy increases, 1 mark for more frequent collisions, 1 mark for more particles exceeding activation energy, 1 mark for more successful collisions/increased rate

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused concentration with surface area' — always clearly distinguish between these factors and explain that concentration refers to amount of solute per unit volume

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to mention activation energy when explaining temperature effects' — always include that more particles exceed activation energy threshold when temperature increases
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was stating catalysts are used up in reactions' — remember that catalysts are not consumed and can be recovered unchanged at the end
note_data.keyDiagram: Rate of reaction graph with two curves on same axes: x-axis shows 'Time (s)', y-axis shows 'Volume of gas produced (cm³)' or 'Mass lost (g)'. Draw one steep curve (higher temperature/concentration) and one gentler curve (lower temperature/concentration). Both curves must level off at same final value but steep curve reaches it faster. Label curves clearly and mark initial rate gradients with tangent lines.
note_data.quickRecap: Rate of reaction measures how fast reactants turn into products. Four main factors increase rate: higher temperature (more kinetic energy), higher concentration (more particles per unit volume), larger surface area (more exposed particles), and catalysts (lower activation energy). Use collision theory to explain: more frequent and energetic collisions lead to faster reactions.
